title: Why You Flip the Second Fraction to Divide

md: To divide fractions, students learn a chant: **keep, change, flip**. Keep the first, change $\div$ to $\times$, flip the second.
It works — but why would *flipping* a fraction have anything to do with dividing? Here are two answers.

blocks.title: The claim
blocks.body: Dividing by $\dfrac{c}{d}$ gives the same result as multiplying by its reciprocal $\dfrac{d}{c}$.

md: Ask a concrete question: how many $\frac{1}{2}$s fit inside $3$? Answer: $6$. So $3 \div \frac{1}{2} = 6$ — and notice $6 = 3 \times 2$. Dividing by $\frac{1}{2}$ *multiplied* by $2$.

Why? Each whole contains $2$ halves, so $3$ wholes contain $3 \times 2$ of them. Dividing by $\frac{1}{d}$ always multiplies by $d$. And dividing by $\frac{c}{d}$ means the pieces are $c$ times bigger, so $c$ times *fewer* fit — divide by $c$. Put together: multiply by $d$, divide by $c$ — that is multiplying by $\frac{d}{c}$.
Here is the airtight version. Write the division as one stacked fraction:
We're allowed to multiply the top and bottom of a fraction by the **same thing** without changing its value. Choose that thing cleverly: multiply by $\dfrac{d}{c}$ — the reciprocal of the bottom.
The bottom was *designed* to collapse: any fraction times its reciprocal is $1$, since $\dfrac{c}{d}\times\dfrac{d}{c} = \dfrac{cd}{dc} = 1$. With a denominator of $1$, only the top survives:
"Flip and multiply" is just the slickest way to make the dividing fraction turn into $1$. The widget below shows the *multiplication* this turns into — the easy part.

blocks.title: The takeaway
blocks.body: The reciprocal isn't pulled from nowhere — it's the exact number that turns the divisor into $1$, and dividing by $1$ does nothing. That's the whole trick.
